title: Question Four
text: Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see https://quarto.org.

title: Question Four
text:
Running Code
When you click the Render button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

1 + 1

[1] 2


You can add options to executable code like this


[1] 4


The echo: false option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).

title: Question Two
text:
data <- galaxies



This is to obtain the reference runtime

set.seed(123)

B <- 10000
n <- length(data)

system.time({
  med_serial <- numeric(B)
  
  for (i in B) {
    sample_i <- sample(data, n, replace = TRUE)
    med_serial[i] <- median(sample_i)
    
  }
})

   user  system elapsed 
   0.00    0.00    0.02 






cluster <- makeCluster(parallel :: detectCores() - 1)

# bootstrapping
set.seed(123)

system.time({
  long_par <- foreach(i = 1:B, .combine = c, .packages = "MASS") %dopar%{
    sample_i <- sample(galaxies, length(galaxies), replace = TRUE) # bootstrapping
    median(sample_i)
  }
})

Warning: executing %dopar% sequentially: no parallel backend registered


   user  system elapsed 
   1.63    0.02    1.69 

# stop cluster 
stopCluster(cluster)




Sending 10 jobs per worker

chunks <- 10
chunk_size <- B/chunks

cluster <- makeCluster(parallel::detectCores() -1)

registerDoParallel(cluster)
  
system.time({
  par_chunk <- foreach(i = 1: chunks, .combine = c,.packages = "MASS") %dopar% {
    
    replicate(chunk_size, {
      sample_i <- sample(galaxies, length(galaxies), replace = TRUE) # bootstrapping
      median(sample_i)
    })
    
  }
})

   user  system elapsed 
   0.00    0.00    0.21 

stopCluster(cluster)




The baseline bootstrap method performs really well, with a runtime that is \(\approx 0\). Obviously with a larger sample size, it would take longer but I still think it is the best option of the three.


The method with 10000 tasks (workers) takes quite some time in terms of efficiency. It is the least efficient method and mostly because of the overhead that comes with parallelism. There is also a matter of waiting for other tasks to finish as well which leads to some idle workers and overall long runtime.
The method with chunks compared to the one without works more efficiently, but again it still has significantly more runtime than miss sequential.

title: Question One
text:
Using a foreach loop to repeat 100 times.

# generating a rn sample from an exp dist. w/ mean = 1
n <- 100

df <- foreach(i = 1: n, .combine = rbind) %dopar% {
  exp_sample <- rexp(n, rate = 1)
  samp_mean <- mean(exp_sample)
  samp_var <- var(exp_sample)
  c(samp_mean, samp_var)

}

as.tibble(df)

# A tibble: 100 × 2
      V1    V2
   <dbl> <dbl>
 1 1.09  1.29 
 2 0.918 0.718
 3 0.883 0.797
 4 0.923 0.613
 5 0.709 0.664
 6 1.16  1.01 
 7 1.14  1.04 
 8 1.13  1.33 
 9 0.831 0.646
10 1.25  1.05 
# ℹ 90 more rows

title: Question Three
text:
Estimating the coverage of a percentile bootstrap CI

B <- 1000
n <- 50
MC <- 2000
true_mean <- 1

cores <- parallel::detectCores() - 1
cl <- makeCluster(cores)
registerDoParallel(cl)

boot_block <- function(M){

  out <- numeric(M)

  for(i in 1:M){

    x <- rexp(n, rate = 1)

    boot_samples <- matrix(sample(x, n * B, replace = TRUE), nrow = n)
    boot_means <- colMeans(boot_samples)

    CI <- quantile(boot_means, c(0.025, 0.975))

    out[i] <- (CI[1] <= true_mean & CI[2] >= true_mean)
  }

  out
}

# split work across cores
jobs <- rep(MC/cores, cores)

results <- foreach(j = jobs, .combine = c) %dopar% {
  boot_block(j)
}

stopCluster(cl)

coverage <- mean(results)
round(coverage,2 )

[1] 0.93

title: Question Three
text:
Estimating the coverage of a percentile bootstrap CI

B <- 1000
n <- 50
MC <- 2000
true_mean <- 1

cores <- parallel::detectCores() - 1
cl <- makeCluster(cores)
registerDoParallel(cl)

boot_block <- function(M){

  out <- numeric(M)

  for(i in 1:M){

    x <- rexp(n, rate = 1)

    boot_samples <- matrix(sample(x, n * B, replace = TRUE), nrow = n)
    boot_means <- colMeans(boot_samples)

    CI <- quantile(boot_means, c(0.025, 0.975))

    out[i] <- (CI[1] <= true_mean & CI[2] >= true_mean)
  }

  out
}

# split work across cores
jobs <- rep(MC/cores, cores)

results <- foreach(j = jobs, .combine = c) %dopar% {
  boot_block(j)
}

stopCluster(cl)

coverage <- mean(results)
round(coverage,2 )

[1] 0.92

title: Question Four
text:
Question Five

nvec <- 3
len <- 5
# 1. serial
system.time({
  res_replicate <- replicate(nvec, {
    x <- rnorm(len)
    max(x)
  })
})

   user  system elapsed 
      0       0       0 

res_replicate

[1] 2.4158352 0.4595894 0.5747557



# 2. parLapply

cluster <- makeCluster(detectCores() -1)

system.time({
  res_parL <- parLapply(cluster, 1:nvec, function(i){
    
    x <- rnorm(5)
    max(x)
  })
})

   user  system elapsed 
      0       0       0 

stopCluster(cluster)
res_parL

[[1]]
[1] 1.405838

[[2]]
[1] 2.624806

[[3]]
[1] 0.221852



cluster <- makeCluster(detectCores() -1)
registerDoParallel(cluster)

system.time({
  res_foreach <- foreach(i = 1:nvec, .combine = c) %dopar% {
    x <- rnorm(len)
    max(x)
  }
})

   user  system elapsed 
   0.01    0.00    0.03 

stopCluster(cluster)
res_foreach

[1] -0.01467026  1.00920724  1.74239642



Summary
No major difference between the methods, foreach performs the worst, while parLappy performs the best. Still though not as fast as sequential Methods, just because of the overhead.
